---
#title: "Week 1 Section"
format: html
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
resources:
  - Week1_section_download.qmd
---

```{=html}
<style>
.header-row {
  display: flex;
  justify-content: space-between; /* title left, button right */
  align-items: center;
  margin-bottom: 1em; /* spacing below header */
}

.page-title {
  font-size: 2rem;   /* matches H1 style */
  font-weight: 700;
  margin: 0;
}

.download-btn {
  display: inline-block;
  background-color: #5FA2F3;  /* lighter R blue */
  color: white !important;
  padding: 8px 16px;
  border-radius: 8px;
  text-decoration: none;
  font-weight: 600;
  font-size: 14px;
}

.download-btn:hover {
  background-color: #4C93E6;
  color: white !important;
  text-decoration: none;
}
</style>
```

<!-- Flex container aligns title and button with text margin -->

::: header-row
<h1 class="page-title">

Week 1 Section

</h1>

<a href="Week1_section_incomplete.qmd" class="download-btn" download> ⬇ Download Starter File </a>
:::

## Quarto

Quarto enables you to weave together content and executable code into a finished document. To learn more about Quarto see <https://quarto.org>.

## Loading and summarizing the data

```{r}
# Uncomment the next lines if you haven't installed the packages yet:
# install.packages(c("openintro", "dplyr", "ggplot2"))

library(openintro)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)

data(email)
head(email)
```

## 1. How many rows and columns are in the dataset?

```{r}
# Your code here
```

Alternative (optional, using dplyr-style workflow):

```{r}
# Your code here
```

## 2. What types of variables are present (e.g., numeric, categorical)?

```{r}
# Your code here
```

## 3. What does the variable `spam` represent?

Write your answer here.

```{r}
# Summary statistics
summary(email$spam)      # Is the email spam?
```

## 4. What is the median number of characters in an email?

```{r}
# Your code here
```

Alternative with `dplyr`:

```{r}
# Your code here
```

## 5. What proportion of emails are spam?

```{r}
# Your code here
```

Alternative with `dplyr`:

```{r}
# Your code here
```

## Subsetting

-   `email$exclaim_mess` is the numeric variable of interest.

-   `email$spam` is the grouping variable.

-   `mean` or `sd` is the function to apply.

-   `na.rm = TRUE` removes missing values when calculating the mean.

Option 1: By hand

```{r}
# Subset rows to only include spam emails
spam <- subset(email, spam == "1")

# Mean and standard deviation of exclamation marks in spam emails
# Your code here


# Subset rows to only include non-spam emails
not_spam <- subset(email, spam == "0")

# Mean and standard deviation of exclamation marks in non-spam emails
# Your code here
```

Option 2: Using `tapply()`

```{r}
# Mean exclamation marks by spam status
# Your code here

# Standard deviation of exclamation marks by spam status
# Your code here
```

Option 3: Alternative with `dplyr`:

```{r}
# Your code here
```

## 6. On average, do spam emails have more exclamation marks than non-spam emails?

Write your answer here.

## 7. What do the differences in standard deviation between the email types tell you about the differences in variability in exclamation marks?

Write your answer here.

## 8. Create a histogram of the number of characters in emails.

Base R version:

```{r}
# Your code here
```

Alternative with `ggplot2`:

```{r}
# Your code here
```

## 9. Describe the shape, center, and spread of the distribution of number of characters in incoming emails for this email account.

Write your answer here.

## 10. Create a boxplot of number of characters by spam status.

Base R version:

```{r}
# Your code here
```

Alternative with `ggplot2`:

```{r}
# Your code here
```
